channel_name: The Prof G Show · Scott Galloway
video_title: China Gets LOCKED OUT of SpaceX and America's Biggest IPOs  (ft. Ed Elson) | China Decode
gist: SpaceX's record $86B IPO excluded Chinese and Hong Kong investors — driven by a dual decoupling where both Washington's national-security instincts and Beijing's capital-controls push Chinese money toward domestic tech listings. The episode maps the implications for the coming half-trillion-dollar US IPO wave, China's parallel domestic IPO boom (humanoid robotics, DRAM), the structural suppression of Chinese tech valuations, and AI labor disruption where China's regulatory pragmatism may paradoxically give it a long-run advantage.

themes.theme: SpaceX IPO mechanics and market reception

insights.claim: SpaceX raised $86B in its IPO, up from the original $75B target due to excess demand, making it the largest IPO in history by a wide margin.
insights.speaker: Ed Elson
insights.so_what: Demand absorption of $86B in a single offering is a stress test for global equity markets; the fact it cleared comfortably sets the stage for the subsequent IPO wave.
insights.quote: they ended up raising about $86 billion. They were going to raise 75, but then there was so much demand for this thing that they ended up pushing it to $86 billion.

insights.claim: SpaceX was priced at $135/share at issuance, rose ~10-11% on day one, then climbed above 25% over $135 and was up ~30% by Monday June 15th, outperforming Ed Elson's initial 25% first-day prediction.
insights.speaker: Ed Elson
insights.so_what: Strong secondary market performance validates institutional demand but does not resolve lock-up expiration risk over the next six months.
insights.quote: it went up over 25% above $135 and then starting the week um on Monday June 15th it has continued to rise up around 30%.

insights.claim: Ed Elson predicts SpaceX stock will face significant downward pressure over the next six months due to lock-up expirations.
insights.speaker: Ed Elson
insights.so_what: Investors who bought into the IPO hype should watch lock-up expiration dates; early shareholders and employees will create supply overhang.
insights.quote: My bet is no, it won't. I think that these lock up expirations are going to be really tough for the stock.

themes.theme: Chinese investor exclusion from US IPOs

insights.claim: Chinese and Hong Kong investors were excluded from the SpaceX IPO over concerns about US export restrictions on critical technology, not a formal legislative ban but de-facto exclusion by the issuer.
insights.speaker: Alice Han
insights.so_what: This sets a template that could extend to OpenAI and Anthropic IPOs; expect structuring of future national-security-sensitive IPOs to exclude Chinese capital by default.
insights.quote: it was an IPO that investors from China and Hong Kong were shut out of over apparent concerns around US restrictions on exports of critical technology.

insights.claim: No formal US legislation or executive action has explicitly blocked Chinese investors from US capital-market flows into stocks; CFIUS was weaponized for M&A/FDI but equity market participation has not been formally restricted from the US side.
insights.speaker: Ed Elson
insights.so_what: The exclusion of Chinese investors from SpaceX was likely a discretionary underwriter/company decision, not a legal mandate — meaning wealthy mainland Chinese via offshore loopholes may still have participated, and a formal ban could be a future escalation lever.
insights.quote: we haven't seen legislation or even executive action from Washington to really block Chinese investors in the capital markets and the flows going into stocks.

insights.claim: China's securities regulator (CSRC) is tightening rules on offshore brokerages conducting cross-border security operations — specifically cracking down on Tiger Brokers and Futu Holdings — to channel mainland capital into domestic assets rather than US-listed stocks.
insights.speaker: Ed Elson
insights.so_what: Futu and Tiger Brokers face regulatory headwinds in their core China-to-US brokerage business; the policy direction reduces structural demand for US-listed equities from mainland Chinese retail investors.
insights.quote: there was a report earlier this year from CSRC...they were going to tighten uh basically these offshore brokerages that were doing crossber security operations.

insights.claim: The dual decoupling thesis: both the US (via issuer discretion and CFIUS) and China (via CSRC crackdowns on cross-border brokers) are simultaneously restricting cross-border capital flows — each reinforcing the other in a mutually accelerating bifurcation.
insights.speaker: Ed Elson
insights.so_what: The bifurcation of global equity capital markets is structural, not cyclical; investors must build separate China and US market exposure frameworks rather than assuming cross-listing arbitrage opportunities persist.
insights.quote: Beijing is to crack down on crossber flows going into US listed stocks because ultimately they see the capital conflict as being really really important for the broader tech conflict.

insights.claim: The same Chinese investor exclusion logic applied to SpaceX will likely extend to OpenAI and Anthropic IPOs, as the tech war has expanded from semiconductors to AI models and capital flows.
insights.speaker: Ed Elson
insights.so_what: Investors in Chinese funds or via Chinese brokerages should plan for structural exclusion from the most high-profile upcoming US tech IPOs.

themes.theme: The coming US IPO wave and equity supply risk

insights.claim: The US market faces approximately $500B in new equity supply from a pipeline including SpaceX ($86B), Google ($86B equity offering), Nvidia, Amazon, Meta offerings, plus OpenAI and Anthropic IPOs — the critical question is whether there is sufficient demand to absorb this without forcing selling of existing tech holdings.
insights.speaker: Ed Elson
insights.so_what: A half-trillion-dollar equity supply wave is a macro risk for existing tech stock holders; rotation out of incumbent positions to fund new IPOs could pressure the broader tech index.
insights.quote: it'll probably go out to around half a trillion dollars in new equity supply. And that will be the test.

themes.theme: Pentagon Chinese Military Company list — signal vs. substance

insights.claim: The Pentagon updated its Chinese Military Companies (CMC) list to include Alibaba, BYD, and Baidu — giants that are effectively the Chinese equivalents of Amazon, Tesla, and Google. The practical impact is limited: it only bars them from US government contracts and does not prevent them from operating in the US or accessing US markets.
insights.speaker: Alice Han
insights.so_what: The CMC list is primarily a reputational and signaling tool, not a hard operational barrier — but corporate America is over-indexing on it, creating a shadow effect that discourages partnerships with listed Chinese firms even for legitimate business.
insights.quote: all it means is that it prohibits you from doing business with the Pentagon...it doesn't prevent you from operating in the US.

insights.claim: The CMC list's shadow effect on corporate behavior is larger than its legal substance: Hollywood executives and major tech companies are refusing to engage with Chinese AI firms like ByteDance's Jianying (Capcut) not because of legal prohibition but out of fear of White House political backlash.
insights.speaker: Ed Elson
insights.so_what: The chilling effect suppresses legitimate US-China commercial AI collaboration; Chinese firms may be more competitively disadvantaged in the US market by perception than by regulation.
insights.quote: the shadow of this entities listing of these sanctions is greater than the actual list itself in the sense that corporate America...will be worried about doing business with Chinese counterparts.

insights.claim: Sora-equivalent Chinese video AI platform Jianying (ByteDance) is described by Hollywood executives as 'above and beyond' any other company globally in video AI, yet US studios won't touch it due to political risk — indicating significant economic cost to the US creative industry from decoupling.
insights.speaker: Ed Elson
insights.so_what: The tech decoupling is imposing real productivity and creative costs on US industries; the opportunity cost of not accessing best-in-class Chinese AI tools is growing.
insights.quote: a few people told me that they are so uh in awe of what Bite Dance is doing in the video AI space...it is above and beyond what any other company in the world is doing but the their legal team won't allow them to touch it.

themes.theme: Anthropic, export controls, and the White House tech politics

insights.claim: The White House imposed export controls on Anthropic's Fable 5 / Mythos model variant after Amazon flagged security concerns — reportedly motivated by a prior dispute where Anthropic set its own terms for Pentagon/Defense Department engagement, angering the administration.
insights.speaker: Ed Elson
insights.so_what: AI companies need to manage White House political relationships as a distinct risk factor from regulatory compliance; being labeled an 'enemy' of the administration can trigger punitive actions dressed as national-security regulation.

insights.claim: Anthropic announced that no Chinese nationals employed at Anthropic will be allowed to work on the Mythos or Fable 5 models — framed as a response to Washington pressure and mirroring SpaceX's decade-old practice of barring Iranian and Chinese nationals from its facilities.
insights.speaker: Ed Elson
insights.so_what: This institutionalizes nationality-based access controls in frontier AI labs, setting a precedent for OpenAI and others under similar government pressure.
insights.quote: Anthropic has announced that uh no Chinese nationals hired by Anthropic will touch both Mythos and Fable 5.

insights.claim: With David Sacks departing, Scott Bessant (Treasury Secretary, finance background) has taken over much of the AI/tech portfolio in the White House, bringing a stronger focus on financial risk and national-security concerns over Silicon Valley innovation interests.
insights.speaker: Ed Elson
insights.so_what: The shift from Sacks to Bessant as de-facto White House AI voice signals more restrictive, finance/security-first AI policy — unfavorable for AI companies that want permissive deployment and export rules.
insights.quote: David Saxs has left somebody who would have been a strong voice uh lobbying for Silicon Valley open AI and anthropic included. We've got Scott Bessant who's probably taken over.

themes.theme: China's domestic IPO pipeline as capital destination

insights.claim: China's domestic and Hong Kong IPO pipeline includes Unitree (largest humanoid robotics company, recently greenlit listing process), CXMT (DRAM chipmaker), and YMTC (NAND flash), plus approximately 50 robotics companies in various stages of IPO application.
insights.speaker: Ed Elson
insights.so_what: As mainland capital is redirected from US stocks, it is being channeled into a bumper domestic tech IPO crop — creating relative valuation opportunities in Chinese robotics and semiconductor listings that Western investors may be structurally barred from or underweight.
insights.quote: We've got Unitry, China's biggest humanoid robotics company that has just given the go-ahead in terms of starting the listing process. We've also got two big semiconductor companies, CXMT and YMTC.

themes.theme: ByteDance / TikTok valuation and IPO probability

insights.claim: ByteDance is valued at ~$600B in private markets despite estimated 2025 revenue of $186B. If valued at Meta's ~10x sales multiple, it would be a $2T company — implying a 'China tax' discount of over 70% due to geopolitical risk, opacity, and potential regulatory intervention.
insights.speaker: Ed Elson
insights.so_what: The 'China tax' is quantifiable at roughly 70% for ByteDance vs. Meta comps — any political de-escalation or governance improvement could unlock massive value, but the risk remains real.
insights.quote: if you look at like Meta for example, Meta trades at around 10 times sales...this is a $2 trillion company. But in the private markets valued at $600 billion. This is sort of the China tax.

insights.claim: ByteDance is unlikely to IPO within the decade because: (1) it doesn't need cash — highly profitable with capex of ~$70B planned for 2026 vs. Meta's higher spend; (2) an IPO would invite regulatory scrutiny in China and globally; (3) TikTok's algorithm export creates unresolvable complications for Chinese data regulators.
insights.speaker: Ed Elson
insights.so_what: ByteDance will remain private, locking out public-market investors from one of the world's most valuable businesses; the only access is via secondary/private markets at significant valuation premium to intrinsic value.
insights.quote: I don't think Bite Dance will IPO within the decade. I think that they don't need to...they're worried that if they IPO that they'll be subject to more regulatory scrutiny both within China and globally.

insights.claim: ByteDance's Kling AI (photo/video editing) may be carved out for a separate IPO, and Jianying (video editing) is seeing massive Hollywood-level demand despite not being formally accessible to US studios.
insights.speaker: Ed Elson
insights.so_what: Kling AI carve-out IPO is speculative but would be the most accessible play on ByteDance's AI video capabilities for public-market investors.
insights.quote: Cling AI which is a photo editing platform that is getting a huge popularity in China and around the world. There's some speculation that that might actually be carved out for a separate IPO.

themes.theme: Elon Musk wealth and the trillionaire milestone

insights.claim: Elon Musk is now worth over $1 trillion post-SpaceX IPO, representing 3.2% of US GDP — more than twice Rockefeller's peak wealth of 1.5% of US GDP, making him arguably the wealthiest person in history on a relative basis.
insights.speaker: Ed Elson
insights.so_what: The concentration of wealth in a single individual who also controls critical national infrastructure (rockets, AI, social media) is unprecedented in US history and raises systemic governance and conflict-of-interest questions.
insights.quote: He's he's now worth more than a trillion dollars...that is equivalent to 3.2% of US GDP...The richest American in history is known to be widely regarded to be John D. Rockefeller, who at the height of his wealth was worth 1.5% of US GDP.

insights.claim: A Chinese trillionaire will not emerge in the foreseeable future due to: lower stock market valuations vs. US comps, CCP political suppression of extreme wealth (Jack Ma precedent), and structural unwillingness of Chinese platform companies to IPO at full value.
insights.speaker: Ed Elson
insights.so_what: The US-China wealth creation divergence reflects a structural difference in how each system monetizes innovation — this is not a temporary gap.
insights.quote: we will not get a trillionaire in our lifetime ed coming out of China. Uh the stock markets in the mainland are just not at the same market cap levels as we see.

insights.claim: Zhang Yiming (ByteDance co-founder) is China's richest person at ~$93B — far from trillionaire territory and constrained by ByteDance's private status and CCP's 'common prosperity' doctrine that suppresses extreme individual wealth displays.
insights.speaker: Ed Elson
insights.so_what: Chinese tech billionaires face both a valuation ceiling (private company discount) and a political ceiling (CCP intervention risk at extreme wealth levels) — the addressable upside for these founders is structurally capped vs. US peers.
insights.quote: The richest man in China is Jang Yaming, who is the co-founder of Bite Dance...He's worth a reported $93 billion.

themes.theme: AI export controls and the futility of containment

insights.claim: China has successfully circumvented Nvidia chip export restrictions by smuggling high-end chips through third-party intermediaries in the Middle East and Malaysia, and has rerouted trade via Southeast Asia and Latin America to evade US tariffs — demonstrating that technology decoupling is practically very difficult.
insights.speaker: Ed Elson
insights.so_what: Export control effectiveness is systematically overstated; investors in US semiconductor/AI restrictions thesis should discount enforcement as China has demonstrated sophisticated workaround capabilities.
insights.quote: China has been smuggling cutting edge Reuben chips from third party countries in the Middle East in in Malaysia for instance and same with trade.

insights.claim: Approximately 38% of top US AI talent are Chinese nationals (not Chinese-Americans), making talent restrictions a severe double-edged sword — restricting Chinese nationals from AI labs would kneecap US AI development more than it would hurt China.
insights.speaker: Ed Elson
insights.so_what: US AI supremacy is structurally dependent on Chinese national talent; any serious talent-based decoupling would impose massive self-harm on the US AI industry — making it politically appealing but operationally counterproductive.
insights.quote: the plurality uh and that's about 38% of the top talent AI people come from China. They're not Chinese Americans, they're actually Chinese nationals.

insights.claim: The AI arms race is analogous to the nuclear arms race: China will inevitably develop frontier AI capability regardless of US restrictions (as it did with nuclear weapons), so the more important strategic question — which the current US administration is not asking — is what policy response makes sense once China achieves AI parity.
insights.speaker: Ed Elson
insights.so_what: The US AI containment strategy is likely futile in the long run; investment frameworks should assume China reaches frontier AI capability and price in the multipolar AI equilibrium rather than a permanent US monopoly.
insights.quote: China in a parallel universe Elon being Chinese and would he have a become a trillionaire...it's quite obvious that they're going to build it either way. They have immense capabilities...like it's going to happen.

insights.claim: US major tech companies — including some large tech firms at an LA conference — are actively using Qwen (Alibaba's open-source AI model) and other Chinese open-source models because they are cheaper and easier to fine-tune than OpenAI or Claude, demonstrating that AI decoupling has not occurred at the enterprise level.
insights.speaker: Ed Elson
insights.so_what: The corporate-level AI decoupling thesis is overstated; Chinese open-source models are winning US enterprise customers on cost/performance — a trend that will be difficult for the administration to reverse without explicit prohibition.
insights.quote: there's some big tech companies that actively are using Qwen. They're actively using Chinese open source models because it's just cheaper. It's faster. They can fine-tune it really easily compared to open AI or claude.

insights.claim: DeepSeek's model is approximately 96% cheaper than equivalent OpenAI models, and Chinese AI models broadly are cheaper at the model layer, infrastructure layer, and energy layer — creating powerful cost-driven adoption pressure even as geopolitical barriers rise.
insights.speaker: Ed Elson
insights.so_what: The 96% cost advantage of Chinese AI models creates an enormous structural incentive for global adoption that political restrictions alone cannot counter; enterprises outside the US will gravitate to Chinese models rapidly.
insights.quote: deepseeks model is from my understanding it's 96% cheaper than the open AAI model.

themes.theme: AI and labor: China's regulatory advantage

insights.claim: China's official Workers Daily (government-backed National Trade Union mouthpiece) identified three core AI labor threats: (1) digital cloning — companies requiring employees to train AI avatars of themselves tied to performance reviews; (2) AI-as-pretext for illegal firing — Chinese courts have already ruled this constitutes unlawful termination; (3) algorithmic digital overseers — opaque platform algorithms eroding worker rights and pay.
insights.speaker: Ed Elson
insights.so_what: China is ahead of the US in developing a specific legal and regulatory framework for AI-related labor rights — this will reduce domestic political resistance to AI adoption and potentially accelerate Chinese tech deployment.
insights.quote: The three core threats that were identified for workers in the AI era were digital cloning...AI being used as a cover for illegal firing...algorithmic digital overseers.

insights.claim: AI could displace up to 278 million Chinese workers by 2049 — one in three employees currently — according to researchers from Peking University, Nanjing University, and other Chinese institutions. Job postings for college graduates fell 22% in H1 2025 vs. prior year.
insights.speaker: Ed Elson
insights.so_what: China's labor market is more structurally exposed to AI disruption than the US because nearly half its urban workforce is in the flexible gig economy (delivery, ride-hailing) — jobs most vulnerable to humanoid robots, autonomous drones, and robo-taxis.
insights.quote: AI could displace up to 278 million Chinese workers by 2049. That's one in three employees in China currently.

insights.claim: Nearly half of China's urban labor force is in flexible gig employment, making it more structurally exposed to AI/automation disruption than economies with more formal employment — autonomous vehicles (BYD robo-taxis already face Wuhan protests), drones, and humanoid robots directly threaten this workforce.
insights.speaker: Ed Elson
insights.so_what: China's gig-economy concentration is a double-edged sword: rapid automation adoption is commercially viable but politically explosive — expect Chinese government intervention (taxes on robot companies, subsidies) to cushion displacement as it did during the SOE restructuring of the 1990s.
insights.quote: half of the labor force is in this flexible gig economy work...these are the jobs that are I think very easy to cut in the world of autonomous drones, autonomous cars, humanoid robotics.

insights.claim: China's AI enthusiasm rate is ~90% vs. ~40% in the US (KPMG/University of Melbourne survey), reflecting historically faster Chinese technology adoption (WeChat, mobile payments, AVs) and government pro-AI messaging — this public sentiment differential could compound China's AI deployment speed advantage.
insights.speaker: Ed Elson
insights.so_what: Public acceptance is a bottleneck for US AI infrastructure (tens of billions in data centers blocked by local opposition in 2025); China's 90% enthusiasm rate removes this political friction, potentially giving it a deployment speed advantage that offsets its technology gap.
insights.quote: Less than half of Americans said that they were excited about it. It was around 40%. And in China, that number was nearly 90%.

insights.claim: In 2025, approximately $10s of billions in US data center projects were blocked by political pushback, 19 states are considering restricting data center construction, and there is growing support for a moratorium on new data centers — representing a self-inflicted competitive disadvantage driven by absence of AI labor regulation.
insights.speaker: Ed Elson
insights.so_what: The US White House's refusal to regulate AI for worker protection is paradoxically generating more political resistance to AI infrastructure than a proactive regulatory framework would — China's approach of addressing worker concerns may produce less infrastructure opposition over time.
insights.quote: Tens of billions of dollars worth of data centers were blocked in the US in 2025 because of political push back. 19 states are considering restricting the construction of data centers.

insights.claim: China is planning ~$375B equivalent in data center capex by 2030 (roughly half the US's ~$750B planned for 2026 alone), but the headline comparison is misleading because infrastructure costs per unit are materially lower in China — actual computing capacity parity may be closer than capex ratios suggest.
insights.speaker: Ed Elson
insights.so_what: Standard US vs. China AI capex comparisons systematically overstate the US advantage by ignoring cost-per-unit differences; investors and analysts should use gigawatt capacity or compute-unit metrics rather than dollar capex for accurate competitive assessment.
insights.quote: US I think will do at least 750 billion capex alone this year for data centers. China just announced that by 2030 it wants to do about half of that...infrastructure costs are much lower in China.

insights.claim: China's CCP has a demonstrated historical precedent of cushioning structural unemployment — during the 1990s SOE bankruptcies, Premier Zhu Rongji deployed subsidies for laid-off workers and the government transitioned to WTO-driven manufacturing growth. This provides an institutional template for managing AI displacement.
insights.speaker: Ed Elson
insights.so_what: Investors should not assume China's gig economy AI disruption will create sustained social instability — the CCP has proven it can manage structural unemployment transitions; more likely outcome is targeted taxes on AI/robotics companies redirected as worker subsidies.
insights.quote: the government is a responsive organism...I point to that period for people just to show you that the government is a responsive organism.
